summary: Sixth Mughal emperor of India (r. 1658-1707), son and successor of Shah Jahan, who seized the throne in a bloody war of succession against his brothers (imprisoning his father in Agra Fort and executing Dara Shikoh). Clues most often identify him through his reversal of Akbar's religious tolerance: he reinstated the jizya tax on non-Muslims, issued the Hanafi legal code Fatawa-e-Alamgiri, executed the Sikh Guru Tegh Bahadur, and banned syncretic court customs. Known as "Alamgir" ("world-seizer"), he brought the Mughal Empire to its greatest territorial extent through wars against the Deccan sultanates and the Marathas under Shivaji and Sambhaji, though the empire's long decline is often traced to the strain of his reign.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Clues most often identify Aurangzeb simply as the sixth Mughal emperor and successor of his father, Shah Jahan, who was known as "Alamgir" ("world-seizer") for bringing the Mughal Empire to its greatest territorial extent. A memorable minor fact involves the immense personal treasure he lost to piracy.

works.name: War of Succession (1657-1659)
works.description: Aurangzeb seized the throne from his ailing father Shah Jahan in a brutal war against his brothers. He convinced his youngest brother, Murad Baksh, to stake his own claim while ostensibly supporting him, then defeated his eldest brother, Dara Shikoh, at the Battle of Deorai after his ascent was secured by an earlier victory at the Battle of Samugarh. He imprisoned Shah Jahan in Agra Fort and had Dara Shikoh accused of heresy and executed. To atone for the violence of his rise over his siblings, he married his son to his nephew's daughter Salima Begum and his own daughters to his nephews Sipihr Shikoh and Izid Bakhsh.

clues.clue: His ascent to power was completed with his victory at the Battle of Samugarh, after which he was crowned emperor
examples: This ruler's ascent to power was completed with his victory at the battle of Samugarh.

works.name: Religious Policy
works.description: Aurangzeb's reign is most often identified with a sharp reversal of the syncretic tolerance practiced by his great-grandfather Akbar: he reinstated the jizya tax on non-Muslims that Akbar had abolished, and had a compendium of Hanafi Islamic jurisprudence, the Fatawa-e-Alamgiri, compiled by nearly 500 jurists. His farmans banned syncretic court customs such as Nowruz and jharokha darshan (the practice of addressing subjects from an ornate balcony), and he banned the printing of the Islamic profession of faith, the kalima, on coins. He prosecuted the mystic Sarmad Kashani for repeatedly declaring "There is no God," and his reign saw the destruction of numerous Hindu temples, including the building of the Shahi Eidgah in Mathura on the site of a previous temple, in response to the Jat Rebellion led by Raja Ram, who defiled the grave of a previous emperor during his revolt.

works.name: Conflict with the Sikhs
works.description: Aurangzeb executed the ninth Sikh Guru, Tegh Bahadur, for refusing to convert to Islam, allegedly after Tegh Bahadur tried to prevent the forced conversion of Hindus in Kashmir. This drew the lasting antagonism of Tegh Bahadur's son and successor, Guru Gobind Singh, who denounced Aurangzeb in an "epistle of victory," the Zafarnama, written after escaping Aurangzeb's forces at the Battle of Chamkaur and declaring, "I have no trust in your oaths anymore."

works.name: Wars & Territorial Expansion
works.description: Aurangzeb's first major battle was the Siege of Orchha in 1635, part of the Bundela War, which he commanded as field commander; his father gave him a sword afterward from which he took his regnal name. As emperor, he expanded the empire through the Ahom Campaign and a series of wars against the sultanates of the Deccan, while his general Shaista Khan conquered Chittagong from the Portuguese and Arakanese pirates. During his reign, the Mughals also fought Mongol-khanate-backed Tibetan forces at the Battle of Basgo.

works.name: War with the Marathas
works.description: Aurangzeb's chief military rival was the Maratha leader Shivaji, who briefly became his vassal under the Treaty of Purandar before Aurangzeb placed him under house arrest at his court in Agra. Aurangzeb abandoned his father's pluralist policies during a long, ruinous war against the Marathas, which culminated in the beheading of Shivaji's son and successor, Sambhaji, though Maratha resistance continued under Rajaram and Tarabai. The military strain of his campaigns, waged to bring the empire to its greatest extent, overextended the empire and placed hardship on the peasantry.

works.name: Legacy & Decline
works.description: Although the last Mughal emperor was not deposed until 1857, the empire's long decline is often traced back to Aurangzeb's death in 1707, after which Gujarat fell into chaos. His trial of his brother Dara Shikoh became the subject of a literary allegory in Mohsin Hamid's debut novel, Moth Smoke.

comprehensive_summary: Aurangzeb was the sixth Mughal emperor of India, the son and successor of Shah Jahan, who seized the throne in a brutal 1657-1659 war of succession: he convinced his youngest brother Murad Baksh to stake a claim to the throne while ostensibly supporting him, defeated his eldest brother at the Battle of Deorai after securing his ascent at the Battle of Samugarh, imprisoned his father Shah Jahan in Agra Fort, and had his brother Dara Shikoh accused of heresy and executed -- later atoning by marrying his children to his nephews and nieces. His reign is most remembered for reversing the religious tolerance of his great-grandfather Akbar: he reinstated the jizya tax on non-Muslims, commissioned the Hanafi legal code Fatawa-e-Alamgiri from nearly 500 jurists, banned syncretic court customs like Nowruz and jharokha darshan and the printing of the kalima on coins, prosecuted the mystic Sarmad Kashani, and destroyed Hindu temples (building the Shahi Eidgah in Mathura in response to the Jat Rebellion of Raja Ram). His execution of the Sikh Guru Tegh Bahadur for refusing to convert to Islam earned him the lasting enmity of Tegh Bahadur's son Guru Gobind Singh, who denounced him in the Zafarnama after escaping Aurangzeb's forces at the Battle of Chamkaur. Militarily, Aurangzeb's career began at the Siege of Orchha in the Bundela War (from which he took his regnal name), and as emperor he expanded the empire through the Ahom Campaign, wars against the Deccan sultanates, the Battle of Basgo against Tibetan forces, and his general Shaista Khan's conquest of Chittagong. He earned the title "Alamgir" ("world-seizer") for bringing the empire to its greatest territorial extent, largely through a long war against the Marathas: his rival Shivaji briefly became his vassal before being placed under house arrest at Agra, and Aurangzeb later beheaded Shivaji's son and successor Sambhaji. This military overreach placed severe strain on the peasantry, and though the last Mughal emperor was not deposed until 1857, historians often trace the empire's long decline to Aurangzeb's death in 1707, after which Gujarat fell into chaos. He also lost an immense fortune when the pirate Henry Avery captured his treasure ship Ganj-i-Sawai, and his trial of Dara Shikoh later inspired a literary allegory in Mohsin Hamid's novel Moth Smoke.
recursive_suggestions: Mughal Empire -- appeared as its own dense answerline (Din-i Ilahi, Ibadat Khana debates, Ahmad Sirhindi, Bernier) spanning multiple rulers; standalone topic distinct from Aurangzeb personally
Shivaji -- appeared as its own answerline with dedicated content (Afzal Khan duel, escape from captivity, Maratha Confederacy founding); potential standalone figure page
Akbar the Great -- appeared as its own answerline (Din-i-Ilahi, Ibadat Khana, Nine Jewels, Fatehpur Sikri); potential standalone figure page, frequently contrasted with Aurangzeb
Shah Jahan -- appeared as its own answerline (moved capital to Delhi, built Taj Mahal); potential standalone figure page
Babur -- appeared as its own answerline (Mongol/Chagatai ancestry, founding of the Mughal Empire); potential standalone figure page
Dara Shikoh -- recurring figure (Mingling of the Two Oceans, trial/execution) dense enough for a possible standalone entry
Guru Gobind Singh / Guru Tegh Bahadur -- recurring Sikh-history figures with independent clue potential (Zafarnama, martyrdom)
